page_content:
# Passage:
While not diminishing the importance of interpersonal relationships and individual psychology, the social stress model emphasizes the sociocultural dimensions of stress. Advocates of the social stress model propose that there are three aspects of the stress process, which include stressors (for example, unemployment), stress mediators (for example, support networks), and stress outcomes (for example, health disorders). According to the social stress model, sociocultural factors shape each aspect of the stress process. One such factor is an individual’s location within social structures, especially in relation to systems of stratification. An additional socio-cultural factor relevant to the stress process is the social status, and possible stigma, associated with an individual’s social roles.  
In any society, members of the most disadvantaged racial groups are expected to report higher levels of stress than those from more advantaged racial groups. This prediction is understood not only in terms of differences in socioeconomic status (SES) between racial groups, but also in relation to the effects of prejudice and discrimination on disadvantaged racial groups. Some researchers highlight the former, explaining that controlling for SES should reduce the differences in stress, and any associated mental health disparities, among racial groups. Others argue the latter, asserting that racial prejudice and discrimination have independent effects on stress and psychological well-being.  
Regardless of the relative effect of race, research has established that people in lower socioeconomic positions face disproportionate exposure to certain stressors. Such disproportionate exposure, especially when combined with weak stress mediators, can explain persistent mental and physical health disparities.  
---
